sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 48, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 47 of about 61 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The ACTION7 didn't work (done=False, level_completed=False). The green target is still at cols 42-45, and the player is at rows 36-41. The trail is connected to green (adjacent), but ACTION7 didn't lock it in.
Looking at the image, the trail appears to be touching the green target. But ACTION7 didn't work. Maybe the trail needs to be fully connected (overlapping) with the target, not just adjacent.
Wait, looking at the previous successful connection with red: the player was at rows 24-29, red was at rows 25-28, and the trail (at rows 26-27) was within red's rows. The trail extended to col 40, and red was at cols 36-39. So the trail overlapped with red's columns.
For green: the player is at rows 36-41, green is at rows 37-40, and the trail (at rows 38-39) is within green's rows. The trail ends at col 40, and green is at cols 42-45. So the trail doesn't overlap with green's columns.
I need to extend the trail further to reach green's columns. Let me try more RIGHTs. The trail ends at col 40, and green starts at col 42. I need the trail to reach at least col 42.
But earlier, RIGHT didn't extend the trail beyond col 40. Let me try more RIGHTs to see if the trail can extend further.
Actually, wait. Looking at the segmentation output, the trail segments at rows 38-39 go from cols 17-19, 20-22, 23-25, 26-28, 29-31, 32-34, 35-37, 38-40. The last segment ends at col 40. But green is at cols 42-45. So there's a gap at col 41.
But the output also says "Green adjacent to: w (id 34)" and "Green adjacent to: w (id 44)". These are trail nodes that are adjacent to green. Let me check which trail segments these are.
Actually, I think the issue is that the trail segments are 2x2 blocks (2 rows x 2 cols), and they're separated by 1-col gaps. So the trail segments are:
- cols 17-18 (2 cols)
- gap at col 19
- cols 20-21 (2 cols)
- gap at col 22
- ...
Wait, no. The segmentation says cols 17-19, which is 3 cols. But pixels=4, which means 2x2. So the boundary is [[38, 17], [38, 19], [39, 19], [39, 17]], which means the segment occupies rows 38-39 and cols 17-18 (2x2). The boundary points are the corners, so cols 17-19 means the right edge is at col 19, but the segment occupies cols 17-18.
Hmm, actually the boundary is clockwise outer-perimeter corner points. For a 2x2 segment at rows 38-39, cols 17-18, the boundary would be [[38, 17], [38, 18], [39, 18], [39, 17]] or [[38, 17], [38, 19], [39, 19], [39, 17]] depending on how the boundary is defined.
If the boundary is [[38, 17], [38, 19], [39, 19], [39, 17]], then the segment occupies rows 38-39 and cols 17-18 (since the right edge is at col 19, but the segment is 2 cols wide, so it's cols 17-18).
Wait, that doesn't make sense. If the boundary is [[38, 17], [38, 19], [39, 19], [39, 17]], then the segment occupies rows 38-39 and cols 17-18 (2 rows x 2 cols = 4 pixels). The boundary points are the corners of the bounding box, so the right edge is at col 19 (exclusive), meaning the segment occupies cols 17-18.
So the trail segments are:
- cols 17-18
- cols 20-21
- cols 23-24
- cols 26-27
- cols 29-30
- cols 32-33
- cols 35-36
- cols 38-39
The last segment ends at col 39, and green starts at col 42. So there's a gap at cols 40-41.
But the output says green is adjacent to trail nodes. Let me check which trail segment is adjacent to green. If the last trail segment is at cols 38-39, and green is at cols 42-45, then they're not adjacent (gap at cols 40-41).
Wait, maybe the boundary interpretation is wrong. Let me re-read: "boundary (clockwise outer-perimeter corner points as [row, col])". For a 2x2 segment at rows 38-39, cols 17-18, the boundary would be [[38, 17], [38, 19], [39, 19], [39, 17]] if the boundary includes the outer edge. So the segment occupies rows 38-39 and cols 17-18, and the right edge is at col 19 (exclusive).
So the last trail segment is at cols 38-39 (boundary [[38, 38], [38, 40], [39, 40], [39, 38]]), and green is at cols 42-45 (boundary [[37, 42], [37, 45], [40, 45], [40, 42]]). The gap is at cols 40-41.
But the output says green is adjacent to trail nodes. This is confusing. Let me just try pressing ACTION7 again, or try extending the trail further with RIGHT.
Actually, I notice that the previous ACTION7 didn't work. Let me try extending the trail further with RIGHT and then pressing ACTION7.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Trail ends at col 39 (boundary 38-40), green at cols 42-45. Gap at cols 40-41.
# Try extending trail further with RIGHT, then ACTION7.

# First, check current trail end
seg = current_frame.segmentation
trail_end = 0
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ('w', 'g') and n['pixels'] == 4 and 30 < min(b[0] for b in n['boundary']) < 45:
        c_max = max(b[1] for b in n['boundary'])
        if c_max > trail_end:
            trail_end = c_max
print(f"Trail end (boundary max col): {trail_end}")

# Try RIGHT to extend trail
action(['RIGHT'])

# Check trail end again
seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
trail_end2 = 0
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ('w', 'g') and n['pixels'] == 4 and 30 < min(b[0] for b in n['boundary']) < 45:
        c_max = max(b[1] for b in n['boundary'])
        if c_max > trail_end2:
            trail_end2 = c_max
print(f"Trail end after RIGHT: {trail_end2}")

# Try more RIGHTs
for i in range(5):
    action(['RIGHT'])
    seg3 = current_frame.segmentation
    trail_end3 = 0
    for n in seg3['nodes']:
        if n['color'] in ('w', 'g') and n['pixels'] == 4 and 30 < min(b[0] for b in n['boundary']) < 45:
            c_max = max(b[1] for b in n['boundary'])
            if c_max > trail_end3:
                trail_end3 = c_max
    print(f"Trail end after {i+1} more RIGHTs: {trail_end3}")

# Try ACTION7
action(['ACTION7'])
print(f"After ACTION7: done={last_action_result.get('done')}")
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"RIGHT": "Extends trail 2 cols right", "LEFT": "Shrinks trail 2 cols from right", "UP": "Moves player up 6 rows", "DOWN": "Moves player down 6 rows", "ACTION7": "Locks in current target connection"}, "object_taxonomy": {"player": "Pink (M) 6x6 square", "targets": "red (completed), blue, green/N", "trail": "Alternating gray/white, 2 rows at player middle"}, "hud_map": {"hud_bar": "Rows 53-63, progress bar + target order"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Player at rows 36-41, trail at rows 38-39. Trail ends at col 39, green at cols 42-45. ACTION7 didn't work.", "goal_model": "Connect trail to green, lock with ACTION7, then do blue", "recent_findings": ["ACTION7 didn't work for green", "Trail and green may not be fully connected"], "open_questions": ["Why didn't ACTION7 work?"], "current_plan": "Extend trail further with RIGHT, then try ACTION7", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["MOUSE on green", "ACTION7 without full connection"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
